summary: P009 is a pragmatic final-year student who has developed increasingly systematic food delivery habits as his academic and work schedule intensified. Living with parents in London, he occupies an interesting position where his family handles most grocery decisions and cooking, but he has significant autonomy over his personal food spending (£50-75/week budget, mostly on delivery). His approach to food delivery is notably strategic - he maintains subscriptions on both major platforms, actively compares prices across apps, and has clear financial thresholds for switching behavior (£3-4 price difference required). What's particularly revealing is his evolution from someone who 'used to have time' in earlier university years to someone who now orders 2-3 times per week due to packed schedules combining classes, part-time work, and social commitments. His relationship with food delivery sits at the intersection of convenience, social activity, and household dynamics - he orders when he doesn't like what's cooked at home, when alone, or when socializing with friends who are 'too lazy to cook.' Despite being price-conscious, he shows loyalty to Uber Eats due to the cashback mechanism and subscription benefits, suggesting he's learned to game the system rather than simply seeking the cheapest option.
personality_notes: Straightforward communicator who frequently seeks clarification in the online format. Self-aware about his habits (acknowledges high social media usage without defensiveness). Methodical in his approach to comparing prices and maximizing subscription benefits. Shows some indecisiveness around food choices but has developed systematic workarounds.
food_relationship: Food delivery serves as both a convenience solution for his busy schedule and a social activity with friends, while his parents handle most household cooking and grocery decisions.

key_moments.description: Reveals he wasn't initially aware of student subscription options but has since strategically acquired both platforms' student deals
key_moments.significance: Shows he's learned to optimize his delivery spending over time, suggesting students may not be aware of all available benefits

key_moments.description: States clear price threshold of £3-4 difference needed to switch from preferred app
key_moments.significance: Demonstrates that subscription benefits create meaningful loyalty even among price-conscious students

key_moments.description: Admits ordering frequency increased to 'cover the cost of subscription'
key_moments.significance: Suggests subscriptions may drive increased usage rather than just reflecting existing behavior

what_not_said: Specific cultural or dietary preferences despite being Indian
Details about what he actually cooks when he does cook
Specific concerns about delivery app working conditions despite considering becoming a rider
initial_hypotheses: Students living with parents may have different delivery patterns - using it for personal choice/autonomy rather than necessity
Subscription loyalty can override price sensitivity when benefits are clearly understood
Campus marketing and voucher distribution are particularly effective for this demographic
questions_for_coding: How do family food dynamics influence individual delivery decisions?
What role does subscription cashback play in creating ordering habits?
How do students balance price comparison behavior with subscription loyalty?
What makes students aware of and adopt subscription benefits over time?
